contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Re-evaluate the strategy whenever query volume, output order, index identity, memory, mutation, hashability, comparability, updates, or worst-case guarantees change.

items.prompt: A static-key membership service changes its query from exact membership to "return every key between a and b in order." How should the preferred structure change?

options.text: The new ordered-range relation favors sorting or another ordered representation.
Exact and range queries are identical for a hash set.
Keep the same strategy because variable names did not change.
Range output never benefits from preprocessing.

feedback.reason: A small change from equality to range semantics crosses the strategy boundary.
feedback.details: The required relation, not the dataset alone, determines reusable structure.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.keep_hash_only: A plain set cannot locate ordered interval boundaries.

items.prompt: A frequency-report task changes from arbitrary group order to ascending key order. What changes in the comparison?

options.text: Sorting becomes directly useful, or the hash path must add key sorting and include that cost.
Output order never affects strategy.
Hash iteration becomes automatically ascending.
Counts must be removed.

feedback.reason: The hash path can remain correct only with an explicit ordering stage.
feedback.details: A sort-and-run path may now reuse its main preprocessing for the final output.

items.prompt: A duplicate-detection task adds the requirement to return the earliest repeated arrival index. Which direction does this push?

options.text: Toward a hash-based seen-state scan that preserves arrival order and can stop early.
Toward sorting values without original indices.
Duplicate existence and earliest repeated arrival are the same contract.
Toward checking every pair regardless of constraints.

feedback.reason: Adding an online original-position result changes the consumed structure.
feedback.details: The best sorting path would need decoration and complete input, while hashing can answer during the scan.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.toward_sort_values: That loses the earliest arrival identity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_change: The latter is position- and order-sensitive.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_pairs: A seen set serves the online relation directly.

items.prompt: A grouping job originally forbids mutation, but the contract changes to allow reordering and imposes a strict auxiliary-memory cap. What transfer is plausible?

options.text: An in-place sort-and-run strategy may become preferable.
The memory cap makes an O(n)-entry hash table automatically better.
Mutation permission never affects memory strategy.
The result must be weakened.

feedback.reason: Allowing mutation changes the sorting path from copy-plus-sort to direct in-place processing.
feedback.details: That can reverse the memory decision even though the duplicate semantics are unchanged.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.toward_more_hash: The new constraint weakens the hash case.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.no_effect: It can eliminate the need for an O(n) copy.

items.prompt: A sorted report changes into an online stream that must answer seen-before immediately. Which transfer is appropriate?

options.text: Use incremental hash membership state rather than waiting to sort the complete stream.
Delay every answer until the stream ends.
Binary-search values as they arrive without maintaining order.
Compare only the last two arrivals.

feedback.reason: Online response and arrival order create a lookup-state signal.
feedback.details: The former ordered-output structure is no longer the primary result.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_unsorted: The active data is not sorted.

items.prompt: Keys stop providing a compatible hash but still provide a total comparator. What should happen?

options.text: Remove the hash strategy from the legal candidate set and use comparison-based ordering where it satisfies the result.
Use an unrelated pointer hash and preserve semantic equality automatically.
The problem becomes unsolvable.
Key operations never affect candidate legality.

feedback.reason: Transfer begins by rechecking which primitive relations exist.
feedback.details: An asymptotically attractive but illegal hash operation cannot remain the chosen strategy.

items.prompt: A static sorted dataset becomes highly dynamic with frequent insertions and deletions, while queries remain exact membership only. Which direction can become attractive?

options.text: A hash set can maintain equality state with expected incremental updates, avoiding repeated full resorting.
Full resorting after every update is always optimal.
Frequent updates automatically create ordered range queries.
Updates make reusable state impossible.

feedback.reason: Update frequency changes the lifecycle cost of preprocessing.
feedback.details: The exact decision still depends on guarantees, order needs, and available dynamic ordered structures.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.resort_everything: Repeated O(n log n) rebuilds may dominate.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.range_required: The query relation remains exact membership.

items.prompt: A service changes from expected throughput goals to a deterministic worst-case latency requirement under adversarial keys. What transfer may be justified?

options.text: Toward a deterministic comparison-sorting or ordered strategy, unless hashing supplies an explicit protected worst-case guarantee.
Expected O(1) is automatically a deterministic guarantee.
Input control never affects hashing.
Sorting becomes O(1).

feedback.reason: A change in required guarantee can reverse the preferred legal strategy without changing the nominal input size.
feedback.details: Expected and deterministic bounds must not be substituted for one another.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_constant: It remains nonconstant but can have predictable bounds.

items.prompt: An unsorted dataset is replaced by a guaranteed already-sorted dataset, while the task remains grouped ordered output. What transfer is appropriate?

options.text: Drop both hash construction and sorting; scan existing equal runs directly.
Build a table because hashing is always faster.
Pay O(n log n) to recreate the same order.
Sorted input cannot contain duplicates.

feedback.reason: Input preconditions are reusable work.
feedback.details: The best transfer can be to neither preprocessing candidate when the required structure is already supplied.

items.prompt: Which contract changes can reverse a hash-versus-sort decision? Select all that apply.

options.text: Changing arbitrary output to ordered output.
Changing equality lookup to range or successor queries.
Changing mutation permission or auxiliary-memory limits.
Renaming a variable from map to values.

feedback.reason: Strategy transfer follows semantic and resource changes.
feedback.details: Cosmetic implementation changes do not justify a new choice.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.variable_rename: Names do not affect the contract.

items.prompt: After a contract change, which resulting workloads move the decision toward hashing? Select all that apply.

options.text: Immediate seen-before answers in arrival order.
Many arbitrary-order key-count queries on static hashable data.
Frequent exact-membership updates and queries under accepted expected guarantees.
Repeated ordered range reports.

feedback.reason: Hash-favoring transfers introduce equality-heavy, online, or dynamic workloads without ordered output.
feedback.details: Ordered ranges cross the other direction.

items.prompt: Which contract changes move the decision toward sorting? Select all that apply.

options.text: Counts must now be emitted in key order.
The query changes to nearest values by magnitude.
Mutation becomes legal and a strict memory cap disallows a large table.
The earliest repeated arrival must be returned online.

feedback.reason: Sorting-favoring transfers add ordered relations or make its memory tradeoff legal.
feedback.details: Online first-repeat semantics favor hashing.

items.prompt: Which workloads can justify a hybrid representation? Select all that apply.

options.text: Many exact queries plus periodic ordered output.
Frequency updates followed by ordered group emission.
Updates and range queries when both equality and order structures must be maintained.
Exactly one simple membership scan with no reuse.

feedback.reason: Some contracts consume both equality and order strongly enough to justify maintaining both structures.
feedback.details: The doubled memory and update costs must be included.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_scan_only: Maintaining two structures is usually unnecessary.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.membership_and_order: A hash index and sorted key view may both be useful.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.counts_then_sorted_keys: Hash counts plus key sorting is a valid hybrid.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.dynamic_ranges: A hybrid or ordered dynamic structure may be justified.

items.prompt: Which tests demonstrate real transfer rather than memorized labels? Select all that apply.

options.text: Keep the data fixed but change equality lookup into an ordered range query.
Keep the result fixed but change mutation, memory, or guarantee constraints.
Keep semantics fixed but change one query into a large repeated workload.
Rename functions while keeping all semantics and costs fixed.

feedback.reason: Transfer is shown when a controlled change causes a justified strategy update.
feedback.details: Surface vocabulary without semantic change should not affect the decision.

items.prompt: Arrange the general transfer decision process.

elements.text: Identify exactly what changed in result, queries, updates, guarantees, order, indices, memory, or mutation.
Re-evaluate legal key operations and correctness for hashing and sorting.
Recompute complete lifecycle time, peak auxiliary space, and output cost.
Keep or change the strategy with an explicit boundary-based explanation.

feedback.reason: A transfer decision is a delta analysis, not a fresh keyword guess.
feedback.details: The explanation should name the changed boundary that reversed or preserved the choice.

items.prompt: Arrange the transfer from arbitrary-order counts to ordered groups.

elements.text: Record that the original hash map served arbitrary-order key counts.
Add ascending key order to the output contract.
Choose using k, n, memory, mutation, and guarantee assumptions.

feedback.reason: The added ordering stage changes both mechanisms' complete costs.
feedback.details: The best result can depend on the number of distinct keys k.

items.prompt: Arrange the transfer from offline sorted grouping to online first-repeat detection.

elements.text: Recognize that the complete input is no longer available before answers are required.
Make arrival order and earliest repeated position part of correctness.
Use incremental equality membership with original-position metadata.
Return when the first repeated arrival is observed.

feedback.reason: The online contract invalidates the former sort-first workflow.
feedback.details: Hash state becomes useful because it can answer before the stream ends.

items.prompt: Arrange a justification for an unfamiliar hash-versus-sort problem.

elements.text: Name the required equality, ordering, grouping, range, or identity relation.
Name output order, indices, mutation, and guarantee requirements.
Model preprocessing, queries, updates, memory, and output size.
Choose or combine strategies and state the condition that would reverse the decision.

feedback.reason: A transferable explanation exposes the mechanism and reversal condition.
feedback.details: It does not rely on familiar problem names.

items.prompt: One exact-membership query is made on an unsorted array of n values. Compare direct scan, hash-set build plus one query, and sort plus binary search.
constraints: Comparison sorting costs O(n log n).

feedback.reason: For one equality query, a scan and a hash build have the same asymptotic time, but the scan avoids O(n) retained table state.
feedback.details: Sorting is typically unjustified unless order is needed for another part of the contract.

items.prompt: A static n-value dataset receives q exact-membership queries. Compare expected hash preprocessing with sort-and-binary-search preprocessing.
constraints: Expected hash build is O(n) and query O(1).
Sorting is O(n log n) and each binary search O(log n).

sort_time: O(n log n + q log n)
O(n log n + q log n)

sort_time.ordered_workload: O(n log n + q log n)

feedback.reason: For equality-only queries under expected assumptions, hashing has the stronger time expression but uses more auxiliary state.
feedback.details: Existing order, guarantees, mutation, or ordered follow-up queries can change the final decision.

items.prompt: Produce ordered frequency groups from n values with k distinct keys. Compare hash counting plus sorting k keys with sorting all n values and scanning runs.

feedback.reason: The transfer decision can depend on k as well as n.
feedback.details: When k is small, hash-plus-key-sort may be attractive; tight memory or existing order may favor the sorting path.

items.prompt: A dataset undergoes u complete versions, each with n values, and receives q exact queries total. Compare rebuilding a hash set for each version with sorting each version.
constraints: Expected hash rebuild is O(n); hash query is expected O(1).
Each sort is O(n log n); binary-search query is O(log n).

sort_time: O(un log n + q log n)
O(un log n + q log n)

sort_time.resorts_queries: O(un log n + q log n)

feedback.reason: Version churn multiplies preprocessing cost and may reverse a decision based only on static query cost.
feedback.details: Incremental updates, ordered queries, and guarantee requirements would require a richer model.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Re-evaluate the strategy whenever query volume, output order, index identity, memory, mutation, hashability, comparability, updates, or worst-case guarantees change.
